literal: the living one; from √jīv, 'to live, to breathe'
invariant_definition: The living, embodied subject as bearer of agency, enjoyership, bondage, and rebirth. Unlike *ātman*, which asks what the self is in its strictest metaphysical sense, *jīva* foregrounds the self as it moves through *saṃsāra*: associated with body, mind, subtle body, and the continuity of *karma*. The term therefore sits inside a practical and theoretical scaffold: *kartṛtva* and *bhoktṛtva*, the relation between consciousness and *antaḥkaraṇa*, the mechanism by which *karma* adheres and fructifies, and the question whether individuality is intrinsic, derivative, or only apparent. Vedāntic schools agree that some such locus must be admitted if bondage and liberation are to be intelligible; they disagree on whether the *jīva* is really distinct from *brahman*, a real mode of *brahman*, or only *brahman* under limiting adjuncts. Outside Vedānta, the term can move differently again, most notably in Jainism where *jīva* is a real substance literally encrusted by karmic matter. The entry therefore cannot be reduced to 'individual soul' without losing the doctrinal machinery the word ordinarily presupposes.

per_school.definition: In Advaita, *jīva* designates *brahman* as though individualized through beginningless *māyā* and the *antaḥkaraṇa*-*upādhi*, so that awakening is not the production of a new self but recognition of the unborn *advaita* [[MK 1.16-18](cite://gaudapada/mandukya-karika/1.Āgama-prakaraṇa.16-18)]. The plurality of *jīvas* is taught through the *ghaṭākāśa*-dṛṣṭānta: adjunct-conditioned differentiation governs pleasure, pain, and empirical traffic, while the *jīva* is neither a real *vikāra* nor an *avayava* of *ātman* [[MK 3.3-7](cite://gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.Advaita-prakaraṇa.3-7)]. Within post-Śaṅkara analysis, *jīvatva* is the *ābhāsa* or *pratibimba* of consciousness in the internal organ, and *avidyā* is assigned to the *jīvas* rather than to *brahman* itself [[BS 1.1](cite://mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.1.1--opening-prose-on-avidy); [PV p. 65](cite://prakasatman/pancapadika-vivarana/jīvasya brahma-pratibimbatvāpādanam); [SS III.278](cite://sarvajnatman/samksepa-sariraka/3.278)]. Accordingly, liberation is immediate at the rise of self-knowledge, because the entire *saṃsāra*-seed has already been burned away [[IṢ 1.9](cite://vimuktatman/ista-siddhi/1.Jīvanmuktiḥ)].

The *jīva* is a real self distinct from body and carried through divine, human, animal, and vegetal embodiments by the stream of beginningless karma, so that Vedānta's task is to destroy the fear generated by that embodied circulation [[VS §4](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§4)]. Its *svarūpa* is common across selves, free from corporeal differentiations, and characterizable as *jñānānandaika-guṇa*, even though karmic embodiment imposes the diversified conditions of *saṃsāra* [[VS §5](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§5)]. Knowledge of *jīva* and Paramātman is therefore inseparable from *upāsanā*, because the self stands under Nārāyaṇa as *antaryāmin* and reaches its end in worship directed to the supreme Person [[VS §3](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§3); [VS §6](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§6)].

The *jīva* is a dependent reality whose very existence, knowledge, liberation, and bliss all stand under Hari's *anugraha*; it is not self-liberating even by knowledge's own nature [[Anuvy. 1.1.13-15](cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/1.1.13-15)]. Its irreducible place in the real order is fixed by *bhedapañcaka*: the difference of *jīva* from Īśvara, from matter, and from other *jīvas* is beginningless and true [[Anuvy. 1.4.111-112](cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/1.4.111-112)]. Non-difference texts are therefore read through *sādṛśya*, *pradhānatva*, and divine *svātantrya*, never as *svarūpābhidāna*, and the distinction of *jīva* and Lord is reaffirmed in the states of deep sleep and departure [[Anuvy. 2.3.66-69](cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/2.3.66-69); [TD 1.3](cite://raghavendra/tantra-dipika/1.3)].

The *jīva* is not accepted as a mere *pariccheda* or *pratibimba* of the Absolute; *abheda*-texts are retained only with respect to shared *cid-rūpatva*, while the aggregate of *jīvas* stands to Bhagavān through his natural *acintya-śakti* as ray-atoms to the sun [[PS §42](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§42); [PS §43](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§43)]. Because Bhagavān is the *māyā*-āśraya and the *jīva* the one deluded by *māyā*, *bhajana* alone is the proper *abhidheya* and *parama-prema* the established *prayojana* [[PS §44](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§44); [PS §45](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§45)]. In Caitanya's own devotional idiom, the *jīva* confesses itself as Kṛṣṇa's *kiṅkara* rather than as an autonomous self-subsistent subject [[Śikṣāṣṭakam 5](cite://caitanya/shikshashtakam/5)].

The *jīva* is *jñāna-svarūpa*, atomic, distinct in each body, fit for conjunction with and disjunction from embodiment, and wholly dependent on Hari [[DaŚ 1](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/1)]. In lived reality it is bound up with beginningless *māyā*, and the tradition explicitly distinguishes bound, liberated, and devotional conditions together with a plurality of sub-differentiations among selves [[DaŚ 2](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/2)]. Śrīnivāsa formulates the same ontology as an asymmetry of *svādhīna* and *parādhīna*: Brahman is independent, the *jīvātman* dependent, and its *aṃśatva* is to be understood as finite *śakti* and *asvatantratva* rather than as absolute identity or absolute separation [[BS 1.1.1](cite://srinivasa/vedanta-kaustubha/1.1.1); [BS 2.3.42](cite://srinivasa/vedanta-kaustubha/2.3.42)].

The *jīva* is Brahman's own *aṃśa*, and this relation is grounded in scriptural *nānā-vyapadeśa* rather than in any ordinary mereological model available from worldly experience [[BSB 2.3.43-45](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/2.3.43-45)]. Vallabha therefore treats the self as an eternal reality within *jīvaloka*, disclosed by Vedic and smṛti testimony, not as an illusory individuality to be dismissed as merely apparent [[BSB 2.3.43-45](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/2.3.43-45)].

In Sāṃkhya, what later inter-school idiom often calls *jīva* is the *puruṣa*: a plurality of conscious subjects established from enjoyership, superintendence, and the very orientation toward *kaivalya* [[SK 17-19](cite://isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/17-19)]. The commerce of bondage, transmigration, and release belongs strictly to *prakṛti* and its evolutes; the *puruṣa* is neither agent nor patient, but the spectator for whose sake *prakṛti* binds, displays itself, and withdraws [[SK 62-65](cite://isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/62-65)].

Yoga construes the empirical self as the *draṣṭṛ* or *puruṣa* conjoined with the *dṛśya* through *avidyā*: though pure seeing alone, it appears as a witness of mental presentations and enters *bhoga* under that conjunction [[YS 2.17-25](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/2.17-25)]. Vyāsa sharpens the point by saying that *kleśa*, *karma*, *vipāka*, and *āśaya* reside in mind yet are ascribed to puruṣa because it is the *bhoktṛ*; the bound self is thus puruṣa under karmically loaded appropriation, whereas Īśvara is the puruṣa untouched by such appropriation [[YBh 1.24](cite://vyasa/yoga-bhasya/1.24)].

In Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika, the individual self is a real *ātman* distinct from body, senses, and mind, known by its marks: desire, aversion, effort, pleasure, pain, and cognition [[NS 1.1.9-10](cite://aksapada/nyaya-sutra/1.1.9-10)][[VaiS 3.2.4](cite://kanada/vaisesika-sutra/3.2.4)]. Later scholastic synopsis states the point in explicit *jīvātman* idiom: there is one eternal, all-pervasive individual self for each body, distinct from the one omniscient Paramātman, while mind is atomic and privately tied to each self [[TSg 17](cite://annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/17)]. Liberation is the complete cessation of the painful conjunction-complex, described as *apavarga* in Nyāya and as *mokṣa* in Vaiśeṣika [[NS 1.1.22](cite://aksapada/nyaya-sutra/1.1.22)][[VaiS 5.2.20](cite://kanada/vaisesika-sutra/5.2.20)].

Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā does not privilege a Vedāntic *jīva*-vocabulary, but it does posit an enduring ritual subject, the *puruṣa* or *yajamāna*, whom dharma joins to *niḥśreyasa* and whom Vedic injunction alone can orient toward supersensible results [[ŚBh 1.1.2](cite://sabara/shabara-bhasya/1.1.2)]. Later Bhāṭṭa discussion makes the same structure explicit: injunctional language is unintelligible unless there is a conscious doer of action and a later *paralokaphalabhoktṛ*, so ritual theory presupposes a self surviving the performed act and its bodily moment [[ŚV 5.9.11-15](cite://kumarila/sloka-varttika/5.9.11-15)].

What ordinary discourse might call a living self is, in Madhyamaka, sustained only by appropriation through the notions "I" and "mine"; when that *upādāna* ceases, birth itself is exhausted [[MMK 18.4](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/18.4); [MMK 18.5](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/18.5)]. Nāgārjuna's more exact point is that Buddha's teaching deploys both *ātman* and *anātman* conventionally, while reality itself is beyond the paired alternatives of self and not-self, being *nirvikalpa* and *aprapañcita* [[MMK 18.6](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/18.6); [MMK 18.7](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/18.7); [MMK 18.9](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/18.9)]. Accordingly, no independently grounded *jīva* can be admitted at the level of *tattva*, since whatever arises dependently is neither simply identical with nor wholly other than its conditions [[MMK 18.10](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/18.10)].

The manifold attribution of selfhood takes place only within *vijñāna-pariṇāma*; what is ordinarily taken as a *jīva* is a conceptual *upacāra*, not a persisting seer outside cognition's own transformations [[Triṃśikā 1](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/1); [Triṃśikā 17](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/17)]. Vasubandhu therefore reads the teaching as an entry into *pudgala-nairātmya*: from seed and appearing object the sixfold consciousness proceeds, yet no single *draṣṭṛ* or *mantṛ* is found there [[Viṃśatikā 9](cite://vasubandhu/vimsatika/9 and Entry into *Pudgala-Nairātmya*)]. The deeper correction is *dharma-nairātmya* and finally *āśraya-parāvṛtti*, where the imagined selfhood of both person and dharmas is relinquished in supramundane knowledge [[Viṃśatikā 10](cite://vasubandhu/vimsatika/10 and Entry into *Dharma-Nairātmya*); [Triṃśikā 29](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/29); [Triṃśikā 30](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/30)].

In Pratyabhijñā/Trika, what appears as the *jīva* is consciousness under self-contraction: *jīvatā* is constituted by *jñāna* and *kriyā*, yet the self's own lordly nature is occluded by māyā [[ĪPK 1.1.4-5](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1.1.4-5)]. The bound subject is therefore the *pramātṛ* blinded by māyā, enmeshed in *karmabandha* and reduced to the condition of *paśu* when reality appears as divided; liberation is the recovery of innate *aiśvarya* through recognition, not the production of a new self [[ĪPK 3.2.2-4](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/3.2.2-4)].

In Jainism, *jīva* is itself a fundamental *tattva*, not a derivative designation for a temporarily embodied self [[TattvS 1.4](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.4)]. Its defining mark is *upayoga*, and it exists in two principal conditions, *saṃsārin* and *mukta*; bondage and release therefore name real states of a soul-substance rather than mere epistemic appearance [[TattvS 2.8-10](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/2.8-10)].

translator_note: Avoid flattening *jīva* to 'soul' or even to 'individual self'. 'Soul' imports Christian substance-language, while 'individual self' can sound like a modern psychological ego. The Sanskrit term ordinarily means the living bearer of embodiment, agency, and karmic continuity. It is therefore close to neither a bare metaphysical substance nor a merely autobiographical person, and it is not simply interchangeable with *ātman*.
